text: We present the case with laparoscopic parenchyma sparing liver resections for deeper lesions, the so-called diamond technique. The preoperative CT scan shows liver metastasis in segment IVa. These are not subcapsular, but lay deeper in the parenchyma. There were no other pathological findings on the CT scan. Two years earlier, the patient underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid resection for a stage IIIa sigmoid carcinoma.

The patient is positioned in anti-Trendelenburg and tilted thirty degrees to the left, facilitating access to the posterior liver segments. The surgical team positions throw cards in a hockey stick pattern from the scythe width to the right flank, ensuring a working distance of six to seven centimeters. The surgeon begins by mobilizing the liver, transecting the falciform, triangular, and coronary ligaments. The Pringle maneuver is prepared by placing a tape around the hepatoduodenal ligament, which is pulled through a cut section of a drain and led out through the abdominal wall for tightening. After liver mobilization, an intraoperative ultrasound is performed on the liver to detect additional lesions and delineate resection margins.

The surgeon performs parenchyma dissection using Accusa, bipolar forceps, and Ligasure devices. Bleeding control is managed with the Pringle maneuver, while stitches are placed in the specimen for traction to expose the resection plane. Larger vessels are controlled using a vessel loop, which is pulled to facilitate clip placement.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis using a monopolar or bipolar device, potentially combined with a hemostatic agent. A suprapubic incision is made to extract the specimens using an endobag. After extraction, the peritoneum is closed and inspected laparoscopically. A final inspection of the liver is conducted before removing all laparoscopic equipment and desuflating the abdomen.

The postoperative recovery of this patient was uneventful, and the patient was discharged from the hospital on day three postoperatively. Final histopathological analysis showed an R-zero resection in both metastases. In conclusion, laparoscopic parenchyma-sparing liver resection for non-peripheral lesions through the DIAMOND technique can be performed safely and without compromising perioperative and oncological outcomes.
